🎯 PMP Master Cheat Sheet

ECO · PMBOK · Agile · EVM · Exam Rules · Situational Judgment

People 42%
Process 50%
Biz Env 8%
~50% Agile
180 Qs / 230 min
76 sec/Q
🔍
6 Non-Negotiable Exam Rules
1
First Instinct
Only change if you have a SPECIFIC, CONCRETE reason — not a feeling. "I misread X" or "I confused X with Y" = valid. Vague doubt = keep your answer.
2
Complete Solution
Does my answer solve ALL problems — including hidden ones? PMI always embeds a secondary problem. Find the answer that solves both.
3
Assess First
Gather full info before acting — UNLESS there is an imminent irreversible consequence (safety, live breach, knowledge walking out door).
4
Authority Limits
Nobody overrides ethics, safety, or legal. Not sponsor. Not senior leadership. Not cultural norms. Not schedule pressure.
5
Systemic Beats Reactive
When problem is recurring ("third time", "consistently", "pattern"), choose the structural fix over the one-time fix.
6
Double Trap
Don't apply the last question's lesson mechanically to the next one. Each question is fresh context. PMI uses your own learning against you.
Master Decision Framework
1
Imminent irreversible harm?
YES → Act immediately. NO → Continue to Q2.
2
Ethics / Safety / Legal issue?
YES → Ethics wins. Always. No authority overrides it. NO → Continue to Q3.
3
Timeline critically tight already?
YES → Convene right people urgently. NO → Assess independently first.
4
Problem happened repeatedly?
YES → Systemic/structural fix. NO → Immediate resolution.
5
Assessed the situation yet?
NO → Assess first. YES → Choose appropriate action.
6
Answer solves ALL problems?
Check for hidden stakeholder, sponsor, or process element. If not — find the answer that does.
✅ PMI Always Rewards
Proactive over reactive Collaborative over unilateral Data-driven decisions Transparent communication Assess before acting Stakeholder engagement Value delivery focus Servant leadership Preventing recurrence Proper escalation sequence
❌ PMI Never Rewards
Hiding information Blind sponsor compliance Premature escalation Scope creep tolerance Sunk cost decisions Skipping change control Ethics override Self-investigating fraud Ignoring safety Mechanical rule-following
🎯 A.R.T Framework — Core Decision Sequence

For every scenario question, work through this sequence before picking an answer:

A
Assess / Analyze
Identify the root cause first. Collect data, evaluate impact on scope/schedule/budget/quality. Do NOT skip to action.
R
Review
Review the relevant plan (project management plan, risk register, resource management plan, stakeholder engagement plan, etc.).
T
Take Action
Only after A and R — meet with stakeholders, submit change request, implement response, escalate if needed.
⚠️ Exception: Skip A→R if there is imminent irreversible harm (safety breach, key knowledge walking out the door, health hazard). Act immediately.
🔑 Keyword Quick Guide

Spot the keyword in the question → apply the matching strategy

KeywordPMI Expects You To…
Do / Do first / Do nextAssess impact → Review relevant plan → Take appropriate action
Have done / Should have doneIdentify what proactive planning was missing (risk plan, communication plan, stakeholder analysis)
Not do / Not have doneAvoid: ignoring stakeholders, bypassing change control, skipping scope, overlooking risks, hiding info
Never doNever: fire/terminate, withhold payment, bring in unsanctioned consultants, shut down project for minor issues, implement changes without CCB approval
Only / AlwaysBeware — absolute terms are usually wrong unless the question explicitly justifies them
Least likely / Most likelyRank options by PMI priority order (most important = first action per the plan)
FIRST thingUsually: Assess → Review plan → then act. Not escalate immediately.
Pattern / Consistently / AgainStructural/systemic fix, not a one-off solution
⚡ Risk vs Issue — Critical Distinction
RISKISSUE
Uncertain — may happen in futureCertain — has already happened or is happening
Keywords: may / might / could potentiallyKeywords: will / should / has happened
→ Update Risk Register→ Update Issue Log first
Analyze qualitatively then quantitativelyTake action — implement risk response from register
New risk → add to Risk Register (NOT risk management plan)Resolved issue → update Lessons Learned + OPA
Stakeholder issue? → Review stakeholder register, stakeholder engagement plan, or communications plan.
When risk becomes issue: implement the risk response captured in the Risk Register.
📋 Question Type Strategies
Question TypeStrategy
Situational / ScenarioRead LAST paragraph first — that is the real question. Apply A.R.T framework.
Multiple ResponseAll selected answers must be correct. Focus on the most fitting PMI actions.
Drag & Drop / MatchingUnderstand sequence or categorization. Match each term before dragging.
Fill in the BlankFocus on key terms and context clues from surrounding text.
Formula-based (EVM/PERT)If too complex, mark and return. CPI=EV/AC, SPI=EV/PV, EAC=BAC/CPI.
Double NegativeRewrite as positive: "not an ineffective way" = "an effective way".
Absolute TermsAvoid "always" / "never" / "only" unless clearly justified.
Prioritizing (BEST/FIRST)Use A.R.T + Master Decision Framework. Avoid extremes.
Sentiment-basedBest answer shows emotional understanding + practical steps (not one or the other).
Traditional vs AgileLook for keywords: Sprint/Iteration/Incremental/PO = Agile. Baseline/WBS/CCB = Predictive.
🗂️ Situational Quick Reference
SituationPMI Action
Safety / health hazard arisesStop all work immediately. Assess and address the safety risk.
New law / regulatory requirementUpdate risk register. Adjust plans to comply. Submit change request if needed.
Environmental / mandatory regulationStop work → begin change control process. Compliance is non-negotiable.
Vendor delivery delayReview procurement/contract plan → meet with vendor → explore alternatives → audit if needed.
Stakeholder complaint about communicationRe-evaluate the communications management plan or stakeholder engagement plan.
New stakeholder identified mid-projectUpdate stakeholder engagement plan (not just the register).
Project closure sequenceObtain formal approval of deliverables FIRST → then lessons learned → then transition → then disband team.
Need high-level guidanceRefer to Project Charter.
Need detailed guidanceRefer to Project Management Plan.
Estimation requiredTeam provides estimates (SME expertise). PM facilitates — does not estimate alone.
Conflict about resourcesWork with Functional Manager first. PMO is last resort.
Scope item missed → deliverable rejectedSubmit a change request to add the missing scope item.
External threat beyond PM's authorityEscalate to project sponsor.
Client bypasses PM, talks to team directlyMeet with client to reinforce proper communication and escalation procedures.
🔍 ITTOS Phase Keywords

Use these to identify which process group a question is in:

Process GroupKeywords to Spot
InitiatingCharter, authorize, identify stakeholders, business case, kick-off
PlanningPlan, Estimate, Define, Develop, Create (WBS, schedule, budget)
ExecutingManage, Conduct, Implement, Acquire, Direct, Perform
Monitoring & ControllingMonitor, Control, Validate, Measure, Audit, Report
ClosingFinalize, Close, Complete, Sign-off, Archive, Lessons Learned
⏱️ Exam Day Protocol
WhenWhat to do
StartWrite your 6 rules on scratch paper FIRST before Q1
Each questionRead LAST SENTENCE first — that is the real question
AnsweringEliminate 2 wrong options first, then pick between remaining 2
DoubtAsk: "SPECIFICALLY what is wrong with my first answer?"
TimeMax 90 sec/Q. Flag hard ones. Never spend 3 min on 1 Q.
Q90Halfway. Deep breath. Reset mentally. Stay disciplined.
Q140Fatigue zone. Slow down slightly. Trust framework not tired brain.
FinishReview flagged questions only. Do not revisit answered questions.
ECO 2021 — The Actual Exam Blueprint
Download free from PMI.org — more important than PMBOK. Every question maps to one of 3 domains.
FocusManaging and leading the project team effectively
Key tasksConflict management · Servant leadership · Remove impediments · Empower team · Build team · Tuckman model · Negotiate agreements · Virtual team management · Ground rules · Emotional intelligence · Mentor stakeholders
PMI tip: Servant leadership is the default. Empower before directing. Coach before commanding.
FocusManaging the technical aspects of the project
Key tasksExecute with urgency · Manage communications · Assess and manage risks · Engage stakeholders · Manage budget and resources · Manage schedule · Manage quality · Manage scope · Integrated change control · Manage procurement · Knowledge transfer · Project closure
PMI tip: All changes — positive or negative — go through ICC. No exceptions. Value delivery over process compliance.
FocusConnecting projects to organizational strategy and value
Key tasksManage compliance · Deliver benefits and value · Monitor external environment · Support organizational change · Benefits realization · OPA vs EEF · Business case validity · Sunk cost awareness · Project vs Program vs Portfolio
Key mindset: Always ask "Does this project still deliver organizational value?" Strategic alignment beats delivery compliance.
OPA vs EEF — Know the Difference
OPAOrganizational Process Assets — INTERNAL. Templates, lessons learned, historical data, policies, procedures. Your org controls these.
EEFEnterprise Environmental Factors — EXTERNAL. Market conditions, regulations, industry standards, culture, political climate. Your org does NOT control these.
5 Process Groups Flow
Initiating → Planning → Executing → M&C → Closing
M&C runs parallel to ALL other process groups — not just at the end. Data flow: Raw Data → Information → Reports.
ProcessesDevelop Project Charter · Identify Stakeholders
Key outputsProject Charter (formal authorization) · Stakeholder Register (who, interest, influence, impact)
PMI tipSponsor OWNS charter. PM DEVELOPS it. Validate business case before proceeding. No charter = no project.
Key outputsProject Management Plan · WBS (100% rule) · Schedule Baseline · Cost Baseline · Scope Baseline = Performance Measurement Baseline · Risk Register · Communications Plan · Stakeholder Engagement Plan
WBS rules100% rule — captures ALL work, nothing more. Work Package = lowest level, can be estimated and assigned. Deliverable-oriented, not activity-based.
PMI tipPlanning is ITERATIVE — not one-time. All changes to approved plan require ICC. Gold plating = scope creep.
Key processesDirect and Manage Work · Manage Quality · Acquire Resources · Develop Team · Manage Team · Manage Communications · Implement Risk Responses · Conduct Procurements · Manage Stakeholder Engagement
Key outputsDeliverables · Work Performance Data (raw) · Change Requests (through CCB) · Issue Log · Lessons Learned Register
PMI tipMost budget spent here. Scope creep happens here. Protect the baseline. Every change → ICC.
Key processesMonitor and Control Work · Perform ICC · Validate Scope · Control Scope · Control Schedule · Control Costs · Control Quality · Monitor Risks · Control Procurements · Monitor Stakeholder Engagement
Key outputsWork Performance Reports · Change Request decisions (approve/reject/defer) · Forecasts (EAC, ETC) · Validated deliverables
ICC ruleALL changes — positive or negative — go through ICC before implementation. No exceptions ever.
Key outputsFinal product transition · Lessons learned archived · All contracts closed · Formal customer acceptance · Resources released
PMI tipNever skip formal closure. Even cancelled projects must be formally closed. Lessons learned must be archived in OPA.
📋 Change Control & Planning Concepts
Change Control Board (CCB)
A formally chartered group responsible for reviewing, evaluating, approving, deferring, or rejecting changes to the project. The CCB may include the PM, sponsor, key stakeholders, and functional managers. No change should be implemented without CCB approval. All change requests must be documented and logged in the change log regardless of the decision.
Integrated Change Control
The process of reviewing all change requests, approving changes, and managing changes to deliverables, project documents, and the project management plan. Every change must be assessed for impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, and risk. Configuration management ensures deliverable integrity is maintained.
Configuration Management
Tracking and controlling versions of deliverables and documents. Includes: Configuration identification (labeling components), Configuration status accounting (recording current state), Configuration verification and audit (ensuring composition is correct). Prevents unauthorized changes.
Assumption Log
A project document recording all assumptions and constraints identified throughout the project. Created during project charter development and updated continuously. Assumptions are factors considered true without proof; constraints are limitations that restrict options. Both can become risks if proven wrong.
Rolling Wave Planning
An iterative planning technique where near-term work is planned in detail while future work is planned at a higher level. As the project progresses, more detail is added. This is a form of progressive elaboration — the plan is refined continuously as more information becomes available. Common in both predictive and adaptive projects.
Progressive Elaboration
The process of increasing the level of detail in a project management plan as greater amounts of information become available. Not scope creep — progressive elaboration adds detail to existing scope, while scope creep adds uncontrolled changes. Plans are expected to become more refined over time.
10 Knowledge Areas — PMBOK 6
Each has a management plan, processes across process groups, and key outputs.
ICCAll changes → CCB → Approve/Reject/Defer → Update baselines. PM is the integrator — connects all areas.
Data flowWork Performance DATA (Executing) → INFORMATION (M&C analysis) → REPORTS (distributed)
Validate vs ControlValidate Scope = customer ACCEPTS deliverables (Executing). Control Scope = managing CHANGES (M&C).
WBS100% rule. Work Package = lowest level. Gold plating = scope creep. Scope creep = unauthorized additions.
CPMCritical Path = longest path = zero float = minimum project duration
Fast trackingOverlap activities → reduces duration, INCREASES risk
CrashingAdd resources to critical path → reduces duration, INCREASES cost
Lead vs LagLead = start before predecessor finishes (compresses). Lag = mandatory wait (extends).
FloatLS-ES or LF-EF. Time activity can delay without delaying project.
🔢 CPM Node Format — exactly like the exam
Green = Early dates (ES top-left · EF top-right) Blue = Late dates (LS bottom-left · LF bottom-right) Red arrows = Critical Path
📐 Forward Pass → Backward Pass → Float
NodeESEFLSLFFloat (LS−ES)Critical?
A1101100✅ YES
E112517316❌ No
B113011300✅ YES
G1125223611❌ No
F303432362❌ No
C313631360✅ YES
D374537450✅ YES
Formulas:
🔵 Forward Pass (→): ES = max(EF of all predecessors)  |  EF = ES + Duration − 1
🔴 Backward Pass (←): LF = min(LS of all successors)  |  LS = LF − Duration + 1
🟡 Float: LS − ES  (or LF − EF)
⭐ Critical Path: Float = 0 on every node  |  Longest duration path
⚠️ Exam traps:
• Float = 0 = critical — delay it and the whole project slips
• Negative float = already behind schedule
• Multiple critical paths = higher project risk
• Crash cheapest activity on critical path first
• Fast track = overlap (more risk)  |  Crash = add resources (more cost)
ReservesContingency = known unknowns, PM controls, IN baseline. Management = unknown unknowns, Sponsor controls, OUTSIDE baseline.
Cost of QualityPrevention + Appraisal + Internal failure + External failure. Prevention is cheapest long-term.
Key ruleQuality = meeting requirements. Prevention over inspection. Build quality in — don't inspect it in.
Manage vs ControlManage Quality = process audits (Executing). Control Quality = inspect deliverables (M&C).
Rule of 77 consecutive points same side of mean on control chart = out of control even if within limits
RACIResponsible (does work) · Accountable (owns result) · Consulted (two-way) · Informed (one-way)
Brook's LawAdding people to late project makes it later initially. Ramp-up consumes existing team's time.
TuckmanForming→Storming→Norming→Performing→Adjourning. Adding members restarts cycle from Forming.
FormulaChannels = n(n-1)/2. 5 people=10, 10=45, 15=105, 20=190
TypesPush (email) · Pull (intranet) · Interactive (meetings). PM spends ~90% of time communicating.
ThreatsAvoid · Transfer · Mitigate · Accept (Active/Passive) · Escalate
OpportunitiesExploit · Share · Enhance · Accept · Escalate
ReservesContingency = PM controls (known unknowns). Management = Sponsor controls (unknown unknowns).
FP contractsSeller bears risk. FFP = most common. FP-EPA = inflation adjustment. FPIF = incentive fee.
CR contractsBuyer bears risk. CPFF = fixed fee. CPIF = incentive fee. Good for undefined scope.
T&MHybrid. Staff augmentation. Set not-to-exceed clause. Buyer has moderate risk.
Key ruleContract dispute → review contract FIRST. Legal advice before any commitments.
Engagement levelsUnaware → Resistant → Neutral → Supportive → Leading. Goal: move toward Supportive or Leading.
Key rulesEngagement plan is CONFIDENTIAL. Identification is CONTINUOUS. Power/Interest grid for prioritization.
Power / Interest Grid
Low InterestHigh Interest
High PowerKeep Satisfied
e.g. Regulatory body, Exec sponsor		Manage Closely
e.g. Senior executive, Key sponsor
Low PowerMonitor
e.g. Peripheral departments		Keep Informed
e.g. End users, affected community
Exam trap: High power + Low interest = Keep Satisfied (not informed — they don't want constant updates). Low power + High interest = Keep Informed (they care but can't influence). New stakeholder identified → update Stakeholder Engagement Plan (not just the register).
🏢 Organizational Structures — PM Authority
StructurePM AuthorityPM RoleWho Manages?
Functional / OrganicLittle or nonePart-timeFunctional Manager
Matrix — WeakLowPart-timeFunctional Manager
Matrix — BalancedLow to moderatePart-timeMixed
Matrix — StrongModerate to highFull-timeProject Manager
Project-orientedHigh to almost totalFull-timeProject Manager
Virtual / HybridLow to moderate / MixedFull-time / MixedMixed
Functional org = PM has almost no authority. Project-oriented = PM has full authority. Matrix strength determines the balance.
🔄 ADKAR Change Management Model
StageWhat it meansHow to achieve it
A — AwarenessPeople know WHY change is neededCommunicate the problem and benefits of the new state
D — DesirePeople WANT to support the changeAddress resistance; show personal benefit; engage sponsors
K — KnowledgePeople know HOW to changeTraining, workshops, user guides, role-based coaching
A — AbilityPeople CAN implement the changePilot programs, hands-on support, practice environments
R — ReinforcementChange is SUSTAINEDMonitor adoption, recognize success, refresh training
🎯 MoSCoW Prioritization
CategoryDefinitionExample
Must HaveEssential — cannot function without itSecure login for a banking app
Should HaveImportant but not critical for first releaseBiometric login (Face ID)
Could HaveNice to have — optional enhancementDark mode setting
Won't Have (now)Not in scope for this release — maybe laterIris scan login
🏷️ Common Exam Terminology Traps
TermDefinition / Exam Trap
Gold PlatingAdding features NOT in scope (even if beneficial). Always wrong in PMI world.
SandbaggingInflating estimates to make performance look better later. Unethical.
Student SyndromeDelaying work until just before the deadline.
Parkinson's LawWork expands to fill the time available.
Dropped BatonCritical info not passed during phase transitions → rework and delays.
ROM EstimateUsed at initiation before scope is defined. Range: -25% to +75%.
Definitive EstimateMost accurate — used late in planning. Range: -5% to +10%.
Contingency ReserveFor known-unknown risks. IS in cost baseline. PM can approve use.
Management ReserveFor unknown-unknown risks. NOT in cost baseline. Requires change request to use.
📐 Key Tools & Frameworks
Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)
A grid linking product requirements from their origin to the deliverables that satisfy them. Ensures every requirement adds business value and provides a structure to track throughout the project lifecycle. Used in Validate Scope to verify all requirements are met. Links requirements → design → development → testing.
Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
A hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work. The lowest level is called work packages. If it's not in the WBS, it's not in the project. Created using decomposition technique. The WBS Dictionary provides detailed descriptions of each component. Together with the scope statement and WBS dictionary, it forms the scope baseline.
Critical Chain Method (CCM)
A schedule method that accounts for resource constraints in addition to activity dependencies. Unlike CPM, CCM adds buffers instead of float: Project buffer (protects finish date), Feeding buffer (protects critical chain from non-critical delays). Removes individual activity padding — aggregates it into buffers.
Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix
Tracks current (C) vs desired (D) engagement levels for each stakeholder across 5 levels:
Unaware → Resistant → Neutral → Supportive → Leading
Goal: Move stakeholders from current to desired level through targeted engagement strategies. Key stakeholders like the sponsor should ideally be at "Leading" level.
Salience Model
Categorizes stakeholders using three attributes: Power (ability to influence), Urgency (need for immediate attention), Legitimacy (appropriateness of involvement). Stakeholders with all three = Definitive stakeholders (highest priority). More nuanced than the Power/Interest grid for complex stakeholder environments.
Agile Manifesto — 4 Values
Individuals and interactions
over processes and tools
Working software
over comprehensive documentation
Customer collaboration
over contract negotiation
Responding to change
over following a plan
Right side still has value — left side is valued MORE
RolesProduct Owner (backlog priority, sole authority) · Scrum Master (facilitator, impediments) · Dev Team (self-organizing, 3-9 people)
EventsSprint (1-4 weeks) · Sprint Planning · Daily Standup (15 min) · Sprint Review (inspect product) · Retrospective (inspect process)
ArtifactsProduct Backlog · Sprint Backlog · Increment (potentially shippable)
Key rulesPO owns backlog — no one overrides. Only PO can cancel sprint. Mid-sprint changes go through PO. SM protects team.
Sprint Review = inspect PRODUCT. Retrospective = inspect PROCESS and TEAM. Know the difference.
VelocityAverage story points per sprint — FORECASTING only. NOT a performance metric. Never compare teams.
DoDDefinition of Done — team-agreed criteria for complete. Applies to every story. Quality gate.
Acceptance criteriaPO-defined conditions for a specific story. Different from DoD — story-specific.
SpikeTime-boxed research to reduce uncertainty before estimating
WIP limitKanban — limit work in progress to improve flow. Reduce context switching.
Brook's LawAdding people to late project makes it later initially. Ramp-up time consumes existing team capacity.
1Satisfy customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software
2Welcome changing requirements even late in development
3Deliver working software frequently (weeks not months)
4Business and developers must work together daily
5Build around motivated individuals — give environment and trust
6Face-to-face conversation is most efficient communication
7Working software is the primary measure of progress
8Sustainable development — constant pace indefinitely
9Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design
10Simplicity — maximizing work NOT done — is essential
11Best architectures emerge from self-organizing teams
12Regular reflection on how to become more effective (retrospectives)
Use Predictive whenUse Agile when
Regulated deliverablesSoftware and digital products
Physical constructionInnovation and R&D
Fixed clear requirementsEvolving requirements
Compliance-heavy industryCustomer collaboration critical
Safety-critical systemsFrequent feedback needed
Exam tip: When scenario is ambiguous, lean AGILE unless clearly physical or regulated environment.
🔄 Agile Practices & Concepts (Exam Topics)
Agile Planning Levels (Planning Onion)
Product Vision → Product Roadmap → Release Plan → Iteration Plan → Daily Plan
Each level provides increasing detail. Vision is the "why," roadmap shows features over time, release plan defines what goes into next release, iteration plan is the sprint commitment, daily plan adjusts at standups.
Backlog Refinement (Grooming)
An ongoing activity where the Product Owner and team add detail, estimates, and order to product backlog items. Not a formal Scrum event but typically consumes ~10% of sprint capacity. Items are split, re-estimated, and acceptance criteria are defined so they meet the Definition of Ready for sprint planning.
Agile Charts & Metrics
Burn-down Chart — Shows remaining work vs time in a sprint. Line goes down. If above ideal line = behind.
Burn-up Chart — Shows completed work vs total scope over time. Shows scope changes clearly. Two lines: work done (rising) and total scope.
Cumulative Flow Diagram (CFD) — Shows WIP across states (To Do, In Progress, Done) over time. Wide bands = bottleneck. Used primarily with Kanban.
Velocity Chart — Story points completed per sprint over time. Used for forecasting future sprints.
Technical Practices
Test-Driven Development (TDD) — Write the failing test first → write minimal code to pass → refactor. "Red-Green-Refactor" cycle.
Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) — Define acceptance criteria as behavior specs ("Given-When-Then") before coding. Business analyst writes use cases first.
Continuous Delivery — Build the demo/product first, then add features incrementally. Every increment is potentially shippable.
Pair Programming — Two developers share one workstation: "driver" codes, "navigator" reviews in real-time. Improves quality.
Mob Programming — Whole team works on one task together. One driver, multiple navigators.
Agile Estimation Techniques
Planning Poker — Team uses numbered cards (Fibonacci: 1,2,3,5,8,13…) to estimate story points. Discuss outliers until consensus.
Affinity Estimation — Silently group user stories into similar-sized buckets. Quick for large backlogs.
T-Shirt Sizing — Rough estimates using S, M, L, XL categories. Good for early roadmap planning.
Scaling & Communication
Scrum of Scrums — Coordination meeting where representatives from multiple Scrum teams sync on dependencies and integration issues.
Information Radiator — A highly visible display (physical or digital board) showing real-time project status. Available to all — promotes transparency.
Osmotic Communication — Information flows naturally in co-located teams by overhearing conversations. Reduces need for formal meetings.
Spike — A time-boxed research task to reduce uncertainty or explore a technical question. Not a deliverable — produces knowledge.
Key Agile Concepts
Minimum Viable Product (MVP) — Smallest product increment that tests a business hypothesis and delivers value. Not "minimum quality."
Incremental Delivery — Delivering usable subsets of the product in stages. Each increment adds functionality.
Iterative Development — Refining the product through repeated cycles of feedback and improvement.
Hybrid Approach — Combining predictive (waterfall) and adaptive (agile) methods. Example: fixed phases with agile iterations within each.
Shu-Ha-Ri — Martial arts learning model applied to agile: Shu (follow rules) → Ha (break rules/adapt) → Ri (transcend rules/innovate). Teams progress through these stages.
EV Always Comes First
CPI = EV/AC  |  SPI = EV/PV
Never flip the numerator and denominator
Core Variables
PVPlanned Value — budgeted cost of work SCHEDULED (what you planned to spend by now)
EVEarned Value — budgeted cost of work PERFORMED (value of work actually completed)
ACActual Cost — actual cost of work PERFORMED (what you actually spent)
BACBudget At Completion — total approved budget for the entire project
Performance Indices
CPI — Cost Performance Index
EV / AC
>1.0 = under budget ✅ | <1.0 = over budget ❌ | =1.0 = on budget
SPI — Schedule Performance Index
EV / PV
>1.0 = ahead of schedule ✅ | <1.0 = behind ❌ | =1.0 = on schedule
CV — Cost Variance
EV - AC
>0 = under budget | <0 = over budget
SV — Schedule Variance
EV - PV
>0 = ahead of schedule | <0 = behind schedule
Forecasting Formulas
EAC — at current efficiency rate
BAC / CPI
If current CPI continues for rest of project. Trigger: "based on current performance"
EAC — at budgeted rate (CPI=1.0)
AC + (BAC - EV)
Remaining work done at planned rate. Trigger: "budgeted rate" or "planned rate"
ETC — at current efficiency rate
(BAC - EV) / CPI
Cost to complete remaining work at CURRENT efficiency
ETC — at budgeted rate
BAC - EV
Cost to complete at planned rate (divide by 1.0). Trigger: "budgeted rate"
VAC — Variance At Completion
BAC - EAC
>0 = under budget at completion | <0 = over budget at completion
TCPI — To Complete Performance Index
(BAC - EV) / (BAC - AC)
Efficiency needed to finish within original budget. >1.0 = must work harder than current pace
Key trigger words: "current rate/efficiency" → use CPI | "budgeted/planned rate" → use 1.0 (BAC-EV)
Other Key Formulas
Communication Channels
n(n-1) / 2
5 people=10 | 10 people=45 | 15 people=105 | 20 people=190
PERT — Three Point Estimate
(O + 4M + P) / 6
O=Optimistic M=Most likely P=Pessimistic. Standard deviation = (P-O)/6
Float / Slack
LS - ES or LF - EF
Critical path = 0 float. How long activity can delay without delaying project.
Expected Monetary Value (EMV)
Probability % × Impact $
Used in risk decision trees. Sum all branches for expected outcome.
📈 Additional EVM Concepts
S-Curve
A graphical display of cumulative costs, labour hours, or other quantities plotted against time. Called "S-Curve" because spending typically starts slow, accelerates in the middle, and slows near the end — forming an S shape. Shows planned progress (PV), actual progress (EV), and actual spending (AC) on one chart. Used for visual performance reporting to management.
Three-Point Estimating & PERT
Uses three values: Optimistic (O), Most Likely (M), Pessimistic (P)
Triangular: E = (O + M + P) / 3
PERT (Beta): E = (O + 4M + P) / 6 — gives more weight to most likely
Standard Deviation: σ = (P - O) / 6
Variance: σ² = ((P - O) / 6)²
Used for both cost and duration estimates. PERT is more commonly tested on the exam.
Risk Management Process
Plan → Identify → Qualitative → Quantitative → Plan Responses → Implement → Monitor
Risk = uncertain event with POSITIVE (opportunity) OR NEGATIVE (threat) impact. Both are risks!
Threat Responses — ATMA
Avoid ⭐Eliminate threat — change plan to remove risk entirely. Best for severe threats.
TransferShift impact to third party — insurance, fixed-price contracts. Risk still exists.
MitigateReduce probability or impact — most common. Add testing, redundancy, training.
AcceptActive = contingency plan ready. Passive = no action. Low priority risks only.
EscalateRisk outside project scope — escalate to program or portfolio level.
Opportunity Responses — ESEA
Exploit ⭐Ensure opportunity occurs — assign best resources, eliminate uncertainty
SharePartner with another party to capture opportunity together
EnhanceIncrease probability or positive impact of opportunity
AcceptTake advantage if it occurs — no proactive action needed
Risk Key Terms
ContingencyKnown unknowns. PM controls. IN cost baseline.
Mgmt reserveUnknown unknowns. Sponsor controls. OUTSIDE baseline but inside budget.
WorkaroundUnplanned response to risk with NO prior contingency plan
Residual riskRemaining risk AFTER response strategy applied
Secondary riskNew risk CREATED BY implementing a risk response
Fallback planSecondary response if primary risk response proves inadequate
⚠️ Risk Concepts Deep Dive
Risk Register
The central document listing all identified risks with: description, category, probability, impact, risk score, response strategy, risk owner, triggers, and status. Created during Identify Risks and updated throughout the project. A living document — never "final."
Risk Response Strategies
For Threats (negative risks):
Avoid — Eliminate the threat entirely (change scope, strategy, or schedule)
Mitigate — Reduce probability or impact (add testing, use proven tech)
Transfer — Shift impact to a third party (insurance, warranties, contracts)
Accept — Acknowledge and don't act unless it happens (active: set contingency; passive: deal with it if it occurs)
Escalate — Push to a higher authority when outside PM's control

For Opportunities (positive risks):
Exploit — Ensure the opportunity is realized (assign best resources)
Enhance — Increase probability or impact of the opportunity
Share — Allocate to a third party best able to capture it (joint venture)
Accept — Welcome it if it happens but don't actively pursue
Escalate — Push to higher authority when outside PM's scope
Probability & Impact Matrix
A grid with probability on one axis and impact on the other. Each risk is plotted to determine its risk score = P × I. Risks are classified as Low, Medium, or High. Used during qualitative risk analysis to prioritize which risks need further analysis or response planning.
Expected Monetary Value (EMV)
EMV = Probability × Impact ($)
Used in quantitative risk analysis and decision tree analysis. Threats have negative EMV; opportunities have positive EMV. Sum all EMVs to determine the overall project risk exposure. Example: 30% chance of $50,000 loss = EMV of -$15,000.
Risk Parameters (Exam Favorite)
Beyond probability and impact, PMI tests these additional risk attributes:
Proximity — How close in time the risk may impact the project
Urgency — How quickly the risk response must be implemented to be effective
Connectivity — Whether the risk is related to or caused by other individual risks
Dormancy — Time between a risk occurring and its impact being discovered
These are used in qualitative risk analysis to provide a richer assessment beyond just P×I.
Risk Appetite, Tolerance & Threshold
Risk Appetite — Organization's willingness to accept risk in pursuit of objectives (strategic level)
Risk Tolerance — Degree of uncertainty an entity can withstand (operational range)
Risk Threshold — The specific level above which risk requires a response and below which it can be accepted
These are set by the organization, not the PM.
Risk Terminology
Risk Trigger — Warning sign that a risk event is about to occur. Defined during Plan Risk Responses.
Risk Owner — Person responsible for monitoring the risk and implementing the response plan.
Secondary Risk — A new risk that arises as a direct result of implementing a risk response.
Residual Risk — Risk that remains after responses have been implemented.
Workaround — An unplanned response to a risk that has actually occurred (not pre-planned).
Prompt List — Pre-defined categories used in risk identification workshops (e.g., PESTLE, technical, external).
Conflict Resolution Sequence
Key question: "WHERE in this sequence are we right now?" The scenario always tells you.
1
No attempt yet → Understand separately
Private individual conversations first. Understand each party's underlying interests.
2
Spoken separately → Facilitated joint meeting
Surface root cause together. Collaborative problem-solving with both parties present.
3
Joint meeting failed → Escalate
Escalate to appropriate authority. Not before — never skip steps 1 and 2.
4
Escalation failed → Formal intervention
HR, legal, restructure, reassignment. True last resort only.
Conflict Resolution Methods — Best to Worst
Collaborate ⭐Win-win. Address root cause. Mutually acceptable solution. PMI PREFERRED.
CompromiseBoth parties give something. Partially satisfies both. Second choice.
AccommodateEmphasize agreement, downplay differences. Temporary — does not resolve underlying conflict.
AvoidPostpone or retreat. Never resolves conflict. Cooling-down period only.
Force ⛔ WORSTWin-lose. Impose your view. Damages relationships. PMI LEAST preferred.
Leadership Styles
Servant ⭐Focuses on team needs, removes impediments. PMI preferred for agile contexts.
TransformationalInspires through vision. Drives change and innovation.
SituationalAdapts style to team maturity and task. PMI pragmatic recommendation.
TransactionalReward and punishment. Works for routine tasks only.
DemocraticInvolves team in decisions. Builds buy-in but takes more time.
AutocraticDirective. Crisis only or inexperienced team. Not the default approach.
Motivation Theories
MaslowPhysiological→Safety→Social→Esteem→Self-actualization. Meet lower needs before higher.
HerzbergHygiene factors (salary, conditions) prevent dissatisfaction. Motivators (achievement, recognition) create satisfaction.
McGregor XPeople dislike work, need direction and control — micromanagement justified.
McGregor Y ⭐People are self-motivated and creative. PMI leans Y — empowerment preferred.
McClellandThree needs: Achievement, Affiliation, Power. Different people driven by different needs.
TuckmanForming→Storming→Norming→Performing→Adjourning. Adding members restarts from Forming.
🧠 PM Mindset — Guiding Principles
PrincipleWhat it means in practice
Always be proactiveDon't bypass steps/processes/documentation. Adhere to procedures.
Servant leadershipPrioritize team needs. Empower, don't command.
StewardshipFocus on project AND customer success. Responsible use of resources.
Ethical conductMaintain high standards in ALL activities. No cultural override.
Collaborate with stakeholdersInvolve them in decisions. Never pass problems TO them.
Act on dataAvoid hasty decisions. Never delegate your PM responsibilities.
Corrective actionsTackle deviations head-on. Use management as a last resort.
Continuous improvementLearn from retrospectives and lessons learned.
PM spends ~90% of time communicating. Face-to-face is preferred for conflict resolution. Tailor communication to each stakeholder.
🆚 Traditional PM vs Agile Scrum Master
AspectTraditional PMAgile Scrum Master
LeadershipDirective — assigns tasksServant — team self-organizes
PlanningComprehensive upfront documentationAdaptive, lean, just-enough docs
ChangeFormal CCB change controlPO manages changes in backlog
QualityCustomers verify deliverablesEmbedded in Definition of Done
PerformanceOn time, on budget, as specifiedValue delivered + customer satisfaction
ConflictResolve based on project objectivesValue conflict as learning opportunity
⚔️ Leas' 5 Levels of Conflict
LevelFocusSignal
1 — Problem to SolveFinding a solution together"Let's figure this out."
2 — DisagreementDifferences become visible"You never listen to my ideas."
3 — ContestWinning matters more than solving"I've been here longer than you!"
4 — Fight/FlightStrong emotions, avoidance/aggression"I'm done working with you!"
5 — IntractablePersonal attacks; original issue forgotten"I want you to fail."
PMI tip: Resolve at Level 1 or 2. Level 3+ needs facilitation. Level 5 may need HR/sponsor escalation.
🧩 MBTI Quick Reference
DimensionType AType BPM Impact
Energy SourceExtraversion (E) — group, actionIntroversion (I) — solo, reflectionMeeting format, communication style
Info ProcessingSensing (S) — facts, details, step-by-stepIntuition (N) — big picture, patternsPlanning detail vs strategy focus
DecisionsThinking (T) — logic, fairnessFeeling (F) — empathy, valuesConflict resolution approach
StructureJudging (J) — organized, deadlinesPerceiving (P) — flexible, adaptivePredictive (J) vs Agile (P) preference
Exam tip: J vs P = structured vs flexible. S vs N = detail vs big-picture. T vs F = logic vs empathy in conflict.
🎓 OSCAR Coaching Model
O
Outcome
What is the development goal? Define what success looks like.
S
Situation
Where are they now? Current skills, gaps, and context.
C
Choices & Consequences
What options exist? What happens with each?
A
Actions
What specific steps will they take next?
R
Review
How will you check progress? Set a follow-up checkpoint.
🧠 Motivation Theories & PM Skills
Tuckman's Team Development Model
Forming — Team meets; polite, independent, uncertain. PM: provide direction.
Storming — Conflicts emerge; power struggles, disagreements. PM: facilitate resolution.
Norming — Team establishes ground rules; trust builds, roles clarify. PM: support collaboration.
Performing — High productivity; interdependent, self-organizing. PM: delegate, empower.
Adjourning — Team disbands; members move to new projects. PM: celebrate, capture lessons learned.
Adding a new member resets the team back to Forming.
Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs
From base to peak: Physiological (food, water, shelter) → Safety (job security, health) → Social/Belonging (teamwork, friendship) → Esteem (recognition, respect) → Self-Actualization (reaching full potential). Lower needs must be satisfied before higher ones motivate. PM should understand which level each team member is at.
Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory
Hygiene Factors (if absent, cause dissatisfaction but don't motivate): salary, work conditions, job security, company policies, supervision.
Motivators (create satisfaction and drive performance): achievement, recognition, meaningful work, responsibility, growth opportunities.
Key insight: Fixing hygiene factors prevents unhappiness but doesn't create motivation. Only motivators drive performance.
McGregor's Theory X & Theory Y
Theory X — Workers inherently dislike work, need to be coerced/controlled, avoid responsibility. Leads to micromanagement.
Theory Y — Workers are self-motivated, seek responsibility, creative problem-solvers. Leads to empowerment.
PMI strongly favors Theory Y and servant leadership approaches.
Expectancy Theory (Vroom)
Motivation = Expectancy (effort → performance) × Instrumentality (performance → reward) × Valence (value of reward). If any factor is zero, motivation is zero. People are motivated when they believe their effort will lead to good performance, which leads to a valued reward.
PM Interpersonal Skills
Emotional Intelligence — Recognizing and managing your own emotions and those of others. Self-awareness, self-regulation, empathy, social skills.
Political Awareness — Understanding power dynamics, alliances, and organizational politics to navigate effectively.
Cultural Awareness — Sensitivity to different cultural norms, communication styles, and customs. Critical for global projects.
Negotiation — Reaching agreements through discussion and compromise. Win-win is the goal.
Decision Making — Using data, expertise, and judgment to make project choices. Styles: autocratic, consultative, consensus.
Networking — Building and maintaining professional relationships and contacts.
Active Listening — Fully concentrating, understanding, and responding thoughtfully. Not just hearing words.
Influence — Using persuasion, collaboration, and personal power (not authority) to guide others.
Team Management Tools
RACI Matrix — Responsible (does the work), Accountable (ultimately answerable — only ONE per task), Consulted (provides input — two-way), Informed (kept up-to-date — one-way).
Ground Rules — Agreed-upon behavioral expectations for the team (meeting etiquette, communication norms, conflict handling).
Team Charter — Document defining team values, agreements, communication guidelines, and decision-making procedures.
Colocation — Placing team members in the same physical location. A "war room" setup. Maximizes communication.
Virtual Team Challenges — Time zone differences, cultural misunderstandings, technology barriers, trust building difficulty. Mitigations: clear communication plan, video calls, overlapping hours.
Coaching vs Mentoring
Coaching — Short-term, task-focused skill development. PM helps team members improve specific capabilities. Often during Performing stage.
Mentoring — Long-term developmental relationship. Senior person guides career growth, shares wisdom. Goes beyond the current project.
PMI Code of Ethics — 4 Values
ResponsibilityTake ownership. Report misconduct. Never hide problems. Accountable for decisions.
RespectDignity for all. Cultural sensitivity. No discrimination or harassment.
FairnessTransparent decisions. Disclose conflicts of interest immediately. No favoritism.
HonestyTruth in all communications. Accurate reporting always. No misrepresentation ever.
🚨 Red Flag Words — Answer Is WRONG
Any answer containing these phrases is almost certainly wrong:
"Downplay" "Don't mention yet" "Keep it internal" "Culturally normal" "Sponsor approved" "Document transparently" "Avoid panic" "Wait for more info" "Just this once" "No one will notice"
Authority Override Table
AuthorityCAN overrideCANNOT override
Product ownerBacklog priorityCustomer safety
SponsorProject decisionsEthics, legal, regulatory
Senior leadershipStrategic directionCompliance, public safety
Audit teamInvestigation processPM reporting to sponsor
Local cultureLocal practicesPMI Code of Ethics
Contract termsScope, deliverablesSafety, ethics, regulatory
Ethics Scenario Patterns
BriberyAlways refuse. Escalate to legal and compliance. "Culturally normal" is completely irrelevant.
Hide dataRefuse. Report accurately. Sponsor authority never overrides honesty requirement.
Fraud reportedCannot promise confidentiality for serious misconduct. Route to HR and compliance — do not self-investigate.
Safety vs scheduleSafety ALWAYS wins. No authority, no deadline, no budget overrides safety ever.
Conflict of interestDisclose immediately. Recuse yourself from all affected decisions.
Regulatory grey areaSeek explicit confirmation from regulator before proceeding. Not prohibited does not mean permitted.
🛡️ Ethics Scenarios (Exam Tested)
Gift Policy
PMs should follow their organization's gift policy. In general: Small token gifts (cultural custom) = OK. Expensive gifts that could influence decisions = NOT OK. When in doubt, disclose to management. During procurement, never accept gifts from vendors that could create the appearance of bias. Always maintain objectivity.
Whistleblowing
If you discover unethical or illegal conduct (fraud, safety violations, corruption), you have a duty to report it — even if the person involved is your sponsor or manager. Escalation path: direct supervisor → compliance/ethics office → external authorities if necessary. PMI Code of Ethics mandates honesty and transparency. Failure to report is itself an ethical violation.
Intellectual Property (IP)
PMs must respect ownership of ideas, documents, and proprietary information. This includes: not sharing confidential information from one project with another, respecting NDAs and contractual agreements, giving proper credit for others' work, and protecting organizational process assets. In procurement contexts, protect both your organization's and the vendor's IP according to contract terms.
BrainstormingGenerate ideas — no criticism during generation phase. Quantity over quality first.
InterviewsOne-on-one information from stakeholders and experts
Focus groupsFacilitated group discussion — gather requirements and opinions
SurveysLarge groups, quantitative data. Fast but low depth.
SWOTStrengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats analysis
BenchmarkingCompare to industry standards or similar projects
DelphiAnonymous expert consensus through iterative rounds. Reduces anchoring and groupthink bias.
Root causeFishbone/Ishikawa diagram. Identify underlying cause not just symptoms.
Pareto chart80/20 rule. Bar chart ranked by frequency. Prioritize top causes first.
Control chartProcess over time. UCL/LCL limits. Rule of 7 = seven consecutive points same side = out of control.
Scatter diagramCorrelation between two variables. Does not prove causation.
HistogramDistribution of data — frequency of occurrences across ranges
Decision treeEMV analysis. Multiply probability by impact across all branches. Sum for expected outcome.
Monte CarloSimulation using ranges — probability of completing within budget or schedule
Tornado diagramSensitivity analysis — which risks have most impact. Widest bar = highest sensitivity.
AnalogousBased on similar past project. Top-down. Less accurate. Faster. Early in project when little detail known.
ParametricStatistical — cost per unit multiplied by number of units. More accurate than analogous.
Bottom-up ⭐Estimate each work package and roll up. Most accurate. Most time-consuming.
PERT(O + 4M + P) / 6. Accounts for uncertainty. SD = (P-O)/6.
Planning pokerAgile — team consensus story point estimation. Reduces anchoring bias.
T-shirt sizingAgile relative sizing (XS/S/M/L/XL) for quick initial rough estimation
CPMCritical Path = longest path = minimum project duration = zero float throughout
Fast trackingOverlap sequential activities → reduces duration, INCREASES risk
CrashingAdd resources to critical path → reduces duration, INCREASES cost. Crash cheapest first.
Resource levelingAdjust for resource constraints → may EXTEND project duration
Resource smoothingAdjust activities within float — does NOT extend critical path duration
TypeRisk bearerBest for
FFP (Firm Fixed Price)SellerWell-defined scope
FP-EPASellerLong projects with inflation risk
FPIF (Incentive Fee)SharedEncourage performance targets
CPFF (Cost + Fixed Fee)BuyerR&D, undefined scope
CPIF (Cost + Incentive)BuyerEncourage cost control
T&M (Time and Material)BuyerStaff augmentation
Contract dispute: review contract FIRST before any action or commitment.

⚡ Read Every Morning — 10 Golden Rules
1. Assess before acting — unless imminent irreversible harm
2. Complete solution — solve ALL problems including hidden ones
3. Ethics wins — always, no exceptions, no authority overrides
4. Systemic fix when problem is recurring
5. Trust first instinct — only change with concrete specific reason
6. EV always first — CPI=EV/AC, SPI=EV/PV, never flip
7. All changes go through Integrated Change Control
8. Sponsor owns charter, PM develops it
9. Conflict: private first → joint meeting → escalate
10. Product Owner owns backlog — no one overrides except PO
